card.indications: Prevention of asthma symptoms with inhaled products, allergic rhinitis with nasal spray, allergic conjunctivitis with ophthalmic drops, and management of mastocytosis symptoms with oral concentrate. It is preventive therapy, not a rapid rescue medication.
card.mechanism: Stabilizes sensitized mast cells and inhibits release of histamine and other inflammatory mediators after antigen exposure. It has minimal systemic absorption by most routes.
card.dosing: Schedules are formulation specific. Inhaled nebulizer solution is commonly used four times daily for prevention. Oral concentrate for mastocytosis is commonly given four times daily before meals and at bedtime. Nasal and ophthalmic products may require several daily applications during exposure.
card.routeAdministration: Use regularly for prevention even when symptom-free. It does not treat acute bronchospasm; keep a prescribed rescue inhaler available. Dilute oral concentrate in water as directed and take before meals. Use clean technique for eye and nasal products.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cromolyn or formulation ingredients. Use caution if inhalation causes bronchospasm or if the patient cannot properly use the delivery device.
card.warnings: Acute bronchospasm can occur after inhalation and may be severe. Anaphylaxis and serious hypersensitivity are rare. Improvement may take days to weeks, and abrupt discontinuation can allow symptoms to return.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Throat irritation, cough, wheezing, unpleasant taste, nasal burning or sneezing, headache, eye stinging, nausea, and abdominal discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe bronchospasm, anaphylaxis, angioedema, significant dysphagia, and severe rash.
card.interactions: Clinically significant systemic drug interactions are uncommon because absorption is limited. It may be used with bronchodilators or corticosteroids, but reductions in other therapy must be clinician directed.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom frequency, rescue-inhaler use, peak flow when ordered, technique and adherence, nasal or eye irritation, GI symptoms, and response over several weeks. Observe the first inhaled doses closely in patients prone to bronchospasm.
card.patientTeaching: Use on schedule and do not expect immediate relief. Carry the rescue inhaler prescribed for acute asthma. Demonstrate correct nebulizer, nasal, eye-drop, or oral-concentrate technique. Report worsening wheeze, facial swelling, or trouble breathing.
card.nutrition: Oral concentrate is usually diluted in water and taken about 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime. No general food restrictions apply. Maintain hydration if throat or mouth irritation occurs.

card.indications: Prevention of organ-transplant rejection, treatment of selected severe autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis or psoriasis, and treatment of chronic dry-eye disease with ophthalmic formulations.
card.mechanism: Binds cyclophilin and inhibits calcineurin, reducing transcription of interleukin-2 and activation of T lymphocytes. Ophthalmic products reduce local immune-mediated inflammation and improve tear production in selected patients.
card.dosing: Systemic dosing is individualized by indication, body weight, organ function, trough concentration, formulation, and interacting drugs. Modified and nonmodified oral products are not bioequivalent. IV dosing is lower than oral dosing and is used when oral therapy is not possible.
card.routeAdministration: Give systemic doses at the same times and consistently in relation to meals. Measure oral solution with the supplied device and mix only as directed. Do not interchange formulations without prescriber supervision. IV cyclosporine requires dilution, a controlled infusion, and close monitoring for anaphylaxis.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cyclosporine or formulation components. Certain autoimmune-treatment uses are contraindicated with uncontrolled hypertension, abnormal renal function, malignancy, or concurrent immunosuppressive therapy. Product-specific contraindications apply.
card.warnings: Systemic therapy can cause serious infection, malignancy, nephrotoxicity, hypertension, hepatotoxicity, neurotoxicity, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, and thrombotic microangiopathy. IV formulations may cause anaphylaxis. Frequent blood-level and organ-function monitoring is essential.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Tremor, headache, hypertension, elevated creatinine, hypertrichosis, gingival overgrowth, nausea, diarrhea, hyperlipidemia, hyperkalemia, and low magnesium. Ophthalmic products commonly cause burning or stinging.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute or chronic kidney injury, severe hypertension, seizures, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, serious opportunistic infection, lymphoma or skin cancer, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, and thrombotic microangiopathy.
card.interactions: Cyclosporine is a CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein substrate with many interactions. Azole antifungals, macrolides, grapefruit, and some calcium-channel blockers can raise levels; rifampin, phenytoin, and carbamazepine can lower them. NSAIDs and other nephrotoxins increase renal injury. Potassium-raising drugs increase hyperkalemia.
card.monitoring: Monitor trough concentrations as ordered, serum creatinine, BUN, blood pressure, potassium, magnesium, liver tests, lipids, CBC, infection, neurologic status, gingiva, skin, and cancer screening. Monitor ophthalmic response and ocular infection symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Take the same formulation at the same times and never switch brands or products without approval. Avoid grapefruit and live vaccines. Report fever, reduced urine, severe headache, tremor, seizure, unusual skin lesions, or swelling. Use sun protection and maintain dental care.
card.nutrition: Keep timing consistent with meals. Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice. Limit high-potassium foods or salt substitutes if potassium is elevated. Follow sodium guidance for hypertension and maintain good oral hygiene to reduce gingival overgrowth.
